texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By using a phenomenological approach, model equations incorporating the resistance-in-series concept were established to evaluate concentration polarization in the boundary layer in feed adjacent to the membrane surface in the vapor permeation and separation of volatile organic compound (VOC)/N2 mixtures through poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS). The vapor permeation experiments were carried out at various VOC concentrations in feed, operating temperatures and feed flow rates. Chlorinated hydrocarbons, such as methylene chloride, chloroform, 1,2-dichloroethane and 1,1,2-trichloroethane were used as organic vapor. With decreasing feed flow rate, the permeability coefficients of VOC component were observed to decrease drastically due to concentration polarization while the nitrogen permeability increased slightly in all of the permeations. From the permeation data of the mixtures, the overall mass transfer coefficient, mass transfer coefficient of VOC component across the boundary layer and concentration polarization modulus as a measure of the extent of concentration polarization were determined quantitatively with help of the model equations. From the analysis on the determined model parameters, the boundary layer resistance due to the concentration polarization of VOCs component was found to be more significant when VOCcondensability was greater, VOC content in the feed mixture was higher, or operating temperature was lower. This study seeks to emphasize the importance of the boundary resistance on the vapor permeation of the vapor/gas mixtures with high permeability and high selectivity towards the minor component VOC. © 2002 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved.

Usually, the influence of concentration polarization in feed on the vapor and gas permeation has been often neglected because of their high mobilities. Thus, there have been very few studies [2,3] on the concentration polarization effect on the permeation of VOC/gas mixture so far although numerous researches have been reported on liquid separations [3-6]. Some experimental study on the permeation of VOC mixtures through membrane module [2] reported a complex combination of the influence of support layer and the effects of concentration polarization in both feed and permeate on the permeations of VOC/N2 mixtures through two types of PDMS coated composite membranes, that is a flat sheet and a hollow fiber composite membranes, but it failed to explain selectively the concentration polarization in the feed from the combined effects. Both the selectivity and flux of VOC component were observed to decrease with decreasing feed flow rate in the vapor permeation of VOC/N2 mixtures using silicone coated propylene hollow fibers [3], and the decreasing of both the selectivity and VOCs component flux was explained incorrectly only in terms of lower VOCs content in the flowing bulk feed caused by the more removal of VOC component at lower feed flow rate, even though the concentration polarization was much likely involved in the permeation behavior.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In previous work [7], basic membrane performances were investigated in the vapor permeation of VOC/N2 mixtures through a PDMS membrane at various operating conditions. The vapor permeation was characterized as a sorption selective process, and it yielded very good membrane performance in the permeation and separation of the VOC/N2 mixtures; permeability coefficients of 370-1100 barrer (1 barrer = 10 -10 cm 3 (STP) cm/(cm 2 s cmHg)) and selectivities of 120-350, depending on the kind of VOCs and its content in feed. Also, the concentration polarization of VOC component in the boundary layer adjacent to the membrane surface was investigated in the vapor permeation of the VOC mixtures through the membrane [8]. With decreasing feed flow rate below 200 cm 3 /min, the membrane performance, that is, permeation rate and selectivity were reduced in the permeation of the VOC/N2 mixtures by reducing the solubility of VOC component in the membrane, as a result of concentration polarization occurring in the boundary layer.

texts.text: 2. Theory

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the permeation of diluted VOCs mixtures with N2 through the PDMS membrane, the concentration difference or partial pressure gradients of VOC component can be used as a driving force for the transport of permeant. Due to the higher permeability of VOC through the membrane compared to the permeability of N2, concentration or partial pressure gradients would be developed in the boundary layers adjacent to both the membrane surfaces, which is called concentration polarization. The film model depicted in Fig. 1 is most commonly used to describe concentration polarization, envisaging a thin stagnant layer of feed adjacent to the membrane surface. In this study, in order to rule out any possible concentration polarization in permeate, homogeneous membrane will be employed and permeate pressure be kept as low as possible. texts.label: text
texts.orig: PDMS (GE655, General Electrics) was generously provided by Dongyang Silicone (Seoul, Korea). It was composed of two parts; part A is mainly PDMS oligomers terminated with vinyl groups and part B a mixture of Pt catalyst and PDMS oligomer with active hydrogen. Methylene chloride, chloroform, 1,2-dichloroethane, and 1,1,2-trichloroethane, which were used as VOC vapor, were obtained from Junsei Chemical Co. (Tokyo, Japan) and all of them were an extra pure grade. All chemicals were used without any further purification.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Membrane preparation
texts.text: 3.2. Membrane preparation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Homogeneous membranes were prepared by radical crosslinking between PDMS oligomer (part A) terminated with vinyl groups and a mixture of Pt catalyst and PDMS oligomer (part B) with active hydrogen. A casting solution was prepared by dissolving parts A and B with a ratio of 9:1 in n -hexane. The casting solution was poured into a glass petri dish and then allowed to dry in a fume hood. After curing at room temperature overnight, the membranes were placed in an oven at 110 ◦ C for 2 h to ensure complete crosslinking. The crosslinking reaction took place by an addition reaction in which the active hydrogens attack the vinyl groups under Pt catalyst. The prepared membranes were 130-160 𝛍 m in thickness.
texts.text: Homogeneous membranes were prepared by radical crosslinking between PDMS oligomer (part A) terminated with vinyl groups and a mixture of Pt catalyst and PDMS oligomer (part B) with active hydrogen. A casting solution was prepared by dissolving parts A and B with a ratio of 9:1 in n -hexane. The casting solution was poured into a glass petri dish and then allowed to dry in a fume hood. After curing at room temperature overnight, the membranes were placed in an oven at 110 ◦ C for 2 h to ensure complete crosslinking. The crosslinking reaction took place by an addition reaction in which the active hydrogens attack the vinyl groups under Pt catalyst. The prepared membranes were 130-160 𝛍 m in thickness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The vapor permeation apparatus used in this study is illustrated in Fig. 2, which enable to generate a certain composition of vapor/gas mixture and also measure its permeation transient through membrane precisely, rapidly and directly. From the permeation transient curve, permeability, diffusion coefficient and solubility coefficient could be determined, respectively. The permeation apparatus and the determination principle of the respective parameter from the transient are described well elsewhere [7-9]. It is composed mainly of three constituents: (a) feeding system, (b) membrane cell and (c) measuring and data acquisition system as shown in Fig. 2.
texts.text: The vapor permeation apparatus used in this study is illustrated in Fig. 2, which enable to generate a certain composition of vapor/gas mixture and also measure its permeation transient through membrane precisely, rapidly and directly. From the permeation transient curve, permeability, diffusion coefficient and solubility coefficient could be determined, respectively. The permeation apparatus and the determination principle of the respective parameter from the transient are described well elsewhere [7-9]. It is composed mainly of three constituents: (a) feeding system, (b) membrane cell and (c) measuring and data acquisition system as shown in Fig. 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The feeding system including a vapor generator was designed to produce a certain composition of vapor/gas mixture. The vapor generator was equipped with a chiller, an electrical heater, two mass flow controllers and two pressure regulators so that the composition of mixture was able to be controlled precisely by adjusting the flow rates of the first and second N2 streams, temperature and pressure.
texts.text: The feeding system including a vapor generator was designed to produce a certain composition of vapor/gas mixture. The vapor generator was equipped with a chiller, an electrical heater, two mass flow controllers and two pressure regulators so that the composition of mixture was able to be controlled precisely by adjusting the flow rates of the first and second N2 streams, temperature and pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane cell placed in the heating was made of stainless steel. A feed mixture entered the cell through the center opening, flew radially through the thin channel and left the cell through the side opening. To prevent the feed velocity from being dependent on location on the membrane cell due to the radial flow geometry, the channel was designed to maintain a location-independent feed velocity by making its gap narrow along with feed flow direction. The effective membrane area in the membrane cell was 14.7 cm 2 . Before measuring permeation transient, both the upstream and downstream sides of PDMS membrane loaded in the cell were degassed simultaneously until the pressure in the cell reached below 0.1 Torr. After then, the produced vapor mixture was introduced into the upstream side of the cell and then permeation started. The equipment was specially designed to obtain a step change in feed pressure in the cell from vacuum to the controlled pressure as soon as introducing the vapor mixture, minimizing the retardation of the permeation response.

texts.orig: When permeation happened through the membrane, the PT and MFM produced voltages corresponding with the permeation, respectively. Since both the PT and MFM were hooked up with a computer through a 21-bit interface module for an analog to digital conversion at a sampling rate, the produced voltages could be monitored and displayed in real time by the computer, performing data acquisition. Thus, two kinds of permeation transients could be measured on-line simultaneously; one was from permeate flow rate determined by the MFM and the other one from permeate pressure measured with time by the PT. These two permeation transients were used complementarily to make up for some weak point of each determination of permeation properties [7]. After the data acquisition, a computer program calculated automatically permeability coefficient and diffusion coefficient from the transient curves measured by the MFM and PT, respectively. The maximum resolution of the interface module was 1 𝛍 V, which was sensitive enough to detect a very small instantaneous flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, the permeations of various VOC/N2 mixtures through homogeneous PDMS membrane have been carried out at different operating conditions by using the novel permeation apparatus. Various chlorinated hydrocarbons, i.e. methylene chloride, chloroform, 1,2-dichloroethane, and 1,1,2-trichloroethane were used as VOC component. Feed mixtures used were the chlorinated hydrocarbon/N2 mixtures. VOCs content in feed ranged from 0.3 to 1.5 vol.% and operating temperatures were 35-65 ◦ C. In all of the vapor permeation experiments, feed pressure was kept at 1.5 bar and feed flew through the membrane cell at different rates of 0-600 cm 3 /min to investigate the concentration polarization effect. Also the resistance in permeate adjacent to the membrane surface, if any, was prevented by keeping the permeate pressure below 2 Torr. The flux of respective mixture was determined from its respective permeation transient curve.

In all of the vapor permeation experiments, feed pressure was kept at 1.5 bar and feed flew through the membrane cell at different rates of 0-600 cm 3 /min to investigate the concentration polarization effect. Also the resistance in permeate adjacent to the membrane surface, if any, was prevented by keeping the permeate pressure below 2 Torr. The flux of respective mixture was determined from its respective permeation transient curve.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: VOC content in feed. The measurements were made at high feed flow rate of 600 cm 3 /min to prevent a serious concentration polarization from occurring at feed side. Usually VOC condensability or VOC size is in the order of its critical temperature; C2H3Cl3 > C2H4Cl2 > CHCl3 > CH2Cl2, and a mixture having a higher critical temperature of VOC component has a higher solubility in rubbery membrane [1,7]. In this study, both the flux and selectivity of the VOC/N2 mixtures were in the order of the critical temperature of VOC component in feed. Also, with increasing VOC content in feed, all of the mixture fluxes increased above the pure nitrogen flux, while selectivity did not change so much. In the previous work [7], the permeation of VOC/N2 mixture through the PDMS membrane was found to be affected significantly by the sorption behavior of permeant, being characterized as a sorption-selective process. It was also reported that the PDMS membrane had a very high selectivity towards VOC due to diluting nitrogen component with VOC component selectively absorbed in the membrane and thereby blocking nitrogen permeation. Also, nitrogen permeation could be more blocked in the permeation of a mixture having a higher critical temperature of VOC component by absorbing VOC component more selectively and then more diluting nitrogen component. Thus, the higher selectivity for the mixture with a higher critical temperature of VOC component is obviously attributed to the higher solubility of VOC component and blocking of nitrogen permeation by the absorbed VOC component.

texts.orig: changed with feed flow rate to greater extent in opposite way at higher VOC content. Selectivity changed more at higher VOC content too, as shown in Fig. 4. It reveals that concentration polarization takes place more significantly at higher VOC content in feed. It could be explained in terms of the following three factors; permeation rate, selectivity and the viscosity of feed. As mentioned earlier, high flux and high selectivity cause serious concentration polarization due to a selective permeation through membrane. Feed viscosity can also play an important role in developing concentration polarization across the boundary layer. Since vapor has a higher viscosity than gas, the viscosity of the mixture will increase correspondingly as vapor content in vapor/gas mixture increases. Thus, in the region of slow feed flow, the mass transfer coefficient in boundary layer would be affected negatively by the higher VOC content in feed due to high viscosity of feed, leading to serious concentration polarization in the boundary layer.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, we investigated the important role the boundary layer resistance plays in the vapor permeation under some conditions. A phenomenological approach incorporating the resistance-in-series model was adopted to derive the governing equations to evaluate and demonstrate the boundary layer resistance in feed.
texts.text: In this study, we investigated the important role the boundary layer resistance plays in the vapor permeation under some conditions. A phenomenological approach incorporating the resistance-in-series model was adopted to derive the governing equations to evaluate and demonstrate the boundary layer resistance in feed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The vapor permeations of the chlorinated hydrocarbon/N2 mixtures through PDMS membrane were carried out at various VOCs concentrations in feed, operating temperatures and feed flow rates. From the permeation data of the mixtures, the overall mass transfer coefficient, the mass transfer coefficient of VOC component across the boundary layer and concentration polarization modulus as a measure of the extent of concentration polarization were determined quantitatively with the help of the established model equations. According to the model, the boundary layer resistance due to the concentration polarization of VOCs component was found to be more significant when the condensability of VOC was greater, VOC content in the feed mixture was higher or operating temperature was lower. From the analysis on the ratio of mass transfer resistance in membrane and the boundary layer, it was found that the boundary layer resistance becomes more important relative to the membrane resistance when feed flow rate is slower. Below 200 cm 3 /min of feed flow, the boundary layer resistance could be significantly comparable to the membrane resistance, resulting in reducing VOC permeability and selectivity to VOC component badly. From the analysis on the boundary layer resistance data, it is concluded that the concentration polarization in the boundary layer must be taken into account in the design of a membrane module or process for the vapor permeation through highly selective membrane and/or at low feed flow rate.
texts.text: The vapor permeations of the chlorinated hydrocarbon/N2 mixtures through PDMS membrane were carried out at various VOCs concentrations in feed, operating temperatures and feed flow rates. From the permeation data of the mixtures, the overall mass transfer coefficient, the mass transfer coefficient of VOC component across the boundary layer and concentration polarization modulus as a measure of the extent of concentration polarization were determined quantitatively with the help of the established model equations. According to the model, the boundary layer resistance due to the concentration polarization of VOCs component was found to be more significant when the condensability of VOC was greater, VOC content in the feed mixture was higher or operating temperature was lower. From the analysis on the ratio of mass transfer resistance in membrane and the boundary layer, it was found that the boundary layer resistance becomes more important relative to the membrane resistance when feed flow rate is slower. Below 200 cm 3 /min of feed flow, the boundary layer resistance could be significantly comparable to the membrane resistance, resulting in reducing VOC permeability and selectivity to VOC component badly. From the analysis on the boundary layer resistance data, it is concluded that the concentration polarization in the boundary layer must be taken into account in the design of a membrane module or process for the vapor permeation through highly selective membrane and/or at low feed flow rate.
